food_subgroup: Japanese seafood set meal with rice bowl and fried octopus

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for baked mentaiko scallops or tako karaage specifically. FOODON_00002473 (seafood dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (composite seafood dish) used as closest parent. No exact code exists for this specific Japanese restaurant set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific composite Japanese seafood set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (1 don rice bowl + 3 baked mentaiko scallops + 1 tako karaage portion + 1 canned drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One full set meal comprising a Japanese rice bowl (don), three baked mentaiko scallops, a portion of deep-fried octopus karaage, and one complimentary canned drink, estimated at approximately 650 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Scallops (cooked), Pollock roe (raw), Octopus (cooked), White rice (cooked), Wheat flour batter (fried); supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Japanese rice dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted component averaging: approximately 30% cooked scallops (USDA FDC #175177), 10% mentaiko/pollock roe (USDA FDC #175148), 20% cooked octopus (USDA FDC #175175), 30% cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (USDA FDC #169756), and 10% deep-fried wheat batter coating. Canned drink contribution excluded from per-100g solid-food calculation. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (GI 54, Atkinson et al. 2008) as dominant carbohydrate contributor
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. The dominant carbohydrate source is the Japanese short-grain white rice don (approximately 30% of total weight, ~107 g cooked rice per serving). Cooked white rice has a GI of approximately 54 per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008). Seafood and fried octopus components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Composite GI estimated at 54 (low). Glycemic load calculated as: (GI 54 × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 107.25) / 100 ≈ 57.9. Carbohydrate per serving estimated as 16.5 g/100g × 650 g = 107.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mentaiko (spicy pollock roe), soy-based seasonings, and deep-frying batter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this set meal due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2730 mg per full serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by white rice don component; protein and fat from seafood partially attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this set meal due to its high glycemic load (GL ≈ 58 per serving from the white rice don); substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion size would meaningfully lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from seafood roe and scallops; deep-frying adds saturated and oxidised fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this meal infrequently due to its high sodium load (~2730 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol content from mentaiko roe and scallops; the deep-fried tako karaage component also contributes saturated fat that may adversely affect lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Set B from The Oyster Bank (Funan, Singapore) is a Japanese seafood set meal priced at SGD 49.38, comprising three baked mentaiko scallops, a portion of tako karaage (deep-fried octopus), a Japanese rice bowl (don), and one complimentary canned drink. Mentaiko refers to spicy marinated pollock roe, which is piped over fresh scallops and baked until golden and slightly caramelised, yielding a rich, umami-forward, mildly spicy flavour with a creamy, roe-laden topping over tender, lightly charred scallop flesh. Tako karaage consists of bite-sized octopus pieces coated in seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until crispy on the outside while remaining chewy and tender inside, a popular Japanese izakaya-style preparation. The don component is a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, which serves as the carbohydrate base of the meal. Nutritionally, this set is protein-rich from the seafood components (estimated 11.2 g protein per 100 g), moderate in fat (4.1 g/100 g), and provides meaningful micronutrients including vitamin B12, selenium, iron, and phosphorus from the scallops and octopus. The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content, estimated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g or 2730 mg per full serving, driven by mentaiko seasoning and soy-based sauces. The glycemic load is high per serving (GL ≈ 58) due to the white rice don, though the GI of the overall meal is moderated by the protein and fat from seafood. This meal is not suitable for halal diets and contains shellfish, fish roe, and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite restaurant set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked scallops, pollock roe, cooked octopus, cooked white rice, and fried batter. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice, GI 54) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-specific preparation variability.
notes: 1. This is a composite restaurant set meal; per-100g values are weighted estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries and should not be treated as analytically measured values. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for these seafood components in the source database. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported for this composite in the source database. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero by formulation as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Japanese seafood preparation. 5. GI is estimated at 54 (low) based on the white rice don as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source per the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic_index set to 54 (low category, ≤55 threshold). 6. The canned drink component is excluded from per-100g solid-food nutrient calculations; its contribution to total meal energy and sugar would increase total meal values. 7. Sodium is a primary clinical concern at an estimated 420 mg/100g (~2730 mg per full serving). 8. This meal is not halal-certified; mentaiko and scallops are shellfish/fish roe products. 9. Serving size estimated at 650 g total for the solid food components (excluding canned drink). 10. Source match type is estimated; a laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish would be required for high-confidence nutrient values.
